title: The Accessory Buyer Is Already Here - We're Just Not Selling to Them
subtitle: Meridian Trucks · category whitespace & merchandising analysis · full-diagnostic mode
why_it_matters: Meridian's buyers attach accessories at the same rate as the best benchmark but spend $210 less per buyer doing it ($310 vs $520 median) - and 44% of what they buy, they buy on the internet instead of from us. Closing part of that gap is worth on the order of $130+ per vehicle sold, with most of the fix requiring no new products.

diagnosis.verdict: This is a merchandising problem, not a demand problem.
diagnosis.narrative: Normal attach + low spend + basic mix + high leakage is the signature of an under-merchandised catalog, not weak demand. Nearly two-thirds of Meridian buyers already walk in wanting accessories - they buy cheap protective basics from us and everything more interesting from someone else, somewhere else. Any recommendation built on demand generation would be spending money to create a customer we already have.

composition.headline: We under-sell the categories that grow spend
composition.narrative: Meridian buyers put nearly half their accessory dollars into mats, liners, and guards - the high-attach, low-ceiling bucket. Northstar's buyers put 31% into visible personalization, the bucket that carries their $520 median spend. That 17-point personalization gap is the spend gap.

benchmark_note.headline: Copperline is the cautionary tale: presence isn't behavior
benchmark_note.narrative: Copperline's Speedworks performance sub-brand occupies 25% of its catalog, yet performance is just 7% of what its buyers purchase - and its median spend is $350, barely above Meridian. Shelf presence alone does not move buyers. Northstar's difference is that it merchandises the shift: four named packs sold in the configurator, shop-by-build journeys, accessorized trucks on every model page. Among brands whose customers actually buy personalization, Meridian's do not - and the winning mechanism is merchandising, not ranging more SKUs.

channel.headline: 18 points of recapture headroom
channel.narrative: Meridian captures 34% of its buyers' accessory purchases in its own channel; 44% goes to the internet. Northstar captures 52% at equal attach. These are purchases that already happen, by our buyers, in categories we already stock - the fix is digital merchandising and point-of-order bundles, not product.

storefront_critique.headline: A parts counter, not a store
bullets: Accessories listed alphabetically within part-type categories; the only navigation aid is a fitment filter.
No accessorized trucks anywhere - a buyer cannot see what a Talon looks like with boards, a tonneau, and a bed tent on it.
No bundles, no named packs, no guidance - 33 SKUs presented flat, the $1,249 exhaust listed with the same weight as a $45 door-edge guard.
The configurator sells zero accessories at the moment of highest intent: point of vehicle order.

merchandising_benchmark.headline: The leaders sell the journey, not the part number
merchandising_benchmark.narrative: Vantage shows the default outcome of doing nothing. Copperline shows a half-measure - storytelling for one niche its buyers don't convert on. Northstar runs the full playbook, and its buyers' mix and spend follow.

packs.headline: Three packs, shippable this quarter
packs.narrative: Meridian's buyer base over-indexes on camping/outdoors (124), fishing/hunting (118), and DIY (111). Every SKU below already exists. Pets index at par (97) - a later niche test at most; golf indexes at 72, so golf theming would be a gimmick and we recommend against it.

pricing_note.headline: Pricing is not the problem. One product is.
pricing_note.narrative: On confirmed comparable items Meridian sits in a ±10% band around peers - modestly above discount players, below peers where we compete on spec. No broad pricing action. One flag: the Sport Cat-Back Exhaust at $1,249 is a rebranded aluminized single-tip unit at near-parity with competitors' stainless systems ($1,149-$1,195). A similar price is hiding an inferior product - upgrade or reposition it; don't discount it.

now.detail: On the storefront at bundle pricing, mapped to demonstrated affinities.
Shop-by-build journeys, accessorized Talon imagery on model pages, top sellers surfaced, a guided build flow instead of the alphabetical list.
Led by the packs, at point of vehicle order - the one Now item with an IT dependency; start it first.
Pack pricing plus a fitment guarantee positions the own channel against the internet sellers taking 44% of purchases.

next.detail: Second running-board finish, an 18-20" wheel range, blackout trim, graphics - closing the offer-side gap behind the 17-point mix gap.
Stainless, dual-tip - or exit the price point.
Interior trim first, following the proven Northstar program.

next.action: Test the pet niche only if affinity confirms
next.detail: Explicitly a halo play; never sized as volume.

next_step.headline: One low-cost proof, 90 days
next_step.narrative: Launch the Basecamp Pack and one accessorized-Talon build page, instrumented: attach of included SKUs, bundle take rate, own-channel share vs baseline. In parallel, pull per-SKU sales by channel - the one dataset this analysis lacked.

mapping_resolutions.reason: in-cab comfort/tech adjacency, not protection or utility

comparability_calls: Bed Cargo Divider vs Copperline Bed Cargo Net - rejected: different products
Basecamp Bed Tent vs Northstar Rooftop Tent - rejected: different product classes
18" vs 19" alloy wheel and Class IV vs Class III hitch - adjacent specs, used directionally only
data_flags: All buyer-behavior figures are synthetic demonstration data styled after a syndicated accessory-buyer survey - no figure here is real market data.
Merchandising attributes come from a simulated storefront audit (June 2026).
Dollar sizing is directional throughout; no volume data was supplied.
appendix.figure_types_note: Attach rate = share of buyers purchasing any accessory (the only '% of customers' figure). Purchase mix = share of purchases, sums to 100% per brand. Catalog share = share of SKUs offered (offer-side, not behavioral). Channel share = share of purchases by channel. Affinity index: 100 = population par.
